content.content: CODEOWNERS LICENSE README.md SECURITY.md docker dolt.plugin.zsh go images integration-tests proto --- # Dolt is Git for Data! Dolt is a SQL database that you can fork, clone, branch, merge, push and pull just like a Git repository. Connect to Dolt just like any MySQL database to read or modify schema and data. Version control functionality is exposed in SQL via system tables, functions, and procedures. Or, use the Git-like command line interface to import CSV files, commit your changes, push them to a remote, or merge your teammate's changes. All the commands you know for Git work exactly the same for Dolt. Git versions files. Dolt versions tables. It's like Git and MySQL had a baby. We also built [DoltHub](https://www.dolthub.com), a place to share Dolt databases. We host public data for free. If you want to host your own version of DoltHub, we have [DoltLab](https://www.doltlab.com). If you want us to run a Dolt server for you, we have [Hosted Dolt](https://hosted.doltdb.com). Prefer Postgres instead of MySQL? Try [Doltgres](https://github.com/dolthub/doltgresql), now in its Beta release. [Join us on Discord](https://discord.com/invite/RFwfYpu) to say hi and ask questions, or [check out our roadmap](https://dolthub.com/docs/other/roadmap) to see what we're building next. # Video Introduction [![Dolt Explainer Video](https://img.youtube.com/vi/H2iZy0Cme10/maxresdefault.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H2iZy0Cme10) # What's it for? Lots of things! Dolt is a generally useful tool with countless applications. But if you want some ideas, [here's how people are using it so far](https://dolthub.com/blog/2024-10-15-dolt-use-cases/). ---GO--- ./go/go.mod ./proto/go.mod ./integration-tests/transactions/go.mod ./integration-tests/go-sql-server-driver/go.mod

ResolveTableInsensitive(ctx *sql.Context, root RootValue, tblName TableName) (trueTableName TableName, table *Table, found bool, err error)
	ResolveTable(ctx *sql.Context, root RootValue, tblName TableName) (table *Table, found bool, err error)
}

type SimpleTableResolver struct{}

var _ TableResolver = SimpleTableResolver{}

func (t SimpleTableResolver) ResolveTableInsensitive(ctx *sql.Context, root RootValue, tblName TableName) (trueTableName TableName, table *Table, exists bool, err error) {
	trueTableNameString, exists, err := root.ResolveTableName(ctx, tblName)
	if err != nil || !exists {
		return TableName{}, nil, false, err
	}
	trueTableName = TableName{
		Name:   trueTableNameString,
		Schema: tblName.Schema,
	}
	table, exists, err = root.GetTable(ctx, trueTableName)
	return trueTableName, table, exists, err
}

func (t SimpleTableResolver) ResolveTable(ctx *sql.Context, root RootValue, tblName TableName) (table *Table, exists bool, err error) {
	return root.GetTable(ctx, tblName)
}

// DoltDB wraps access to the underlying noms database and hides some of the details of the underlying storage.
type DoltDB struct {
	db  hooksDatabase
	vrw types.ValueReadWriter
	ns  tree.NodeStore

	// databaseName holds the name of the database for this DoltDB instance. Note that this name may not be
	// populated for all DoltDB instances. For filesystem based databases, the database name is determined
	// by looking through the filepath in reverse, finding the first .dolt directory, and then taking the
	// parent directory as the database name. For non-filesystem based databases, the database name will not
	// currently be populated.
	databaseName string

	// Keep a LRU Cache of materialized commits to speed up future commit resolutions
	commitCache *lru.Cache[hash.Hash, *OptionalCommit]
}

// IsWorkingSetRef reports whether |ref| identifies the working set or staging area rather than a commit.
func IsWorkingSetRef(ref string) bool {
	return ref == Working || ref == Staged
}

// DoltDBFromCS creates a DoltDB from a noms chunks.ChunkStore
func DoltDBFromCS(cs chunks.ChunkStore, databaseName string) (*DoltDB, error) {
	vrw := types.NewValueStore(cs)
	ns := tree.NewNodeStore(cs)
	db := datas.NewTypesDatabase(vrw, ns)
	commitCache, err := lru.New[hash.Hash, *OptionalCommit](commitCacheSize)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, err
	}
	ret := &DoltDB{
		db:           hooksDatabase{Database: db},
		vrw:          vrw,
		ns:           ns,
		databaseName: databaseName,
		commitCache:  commitCache,
	}
	ret.db.db = ret
	return ret, nil
}

// GetDatabaseName returns the name of the database.
// Note: This can return an empty string if the database name is not populated.
func (ddb *DoltDB) GetDatabaseName() string {
	return ddb.databaseName
}

// ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB unwraps a DoltDB to a datas.Database.
// This method should really only be used in cases where we need to get to the internals of the database, such
// as scanning for corruption or migrating data formats. Generally avoid this function.
func ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB(ddb *DoltDB) datas.Database {
	return ddb.db
}

// LoadDoltDB will acquire a reference to the underlying noms db.  If the Location is InMemDoltDB then a reference
// to a newly created in memory database will be used. If the location is LocalDirDoltDB, the directory must exist or
// this returns nil.
func LoadDoltDB(ctx context.Context, nbf *types.NomsBinFormat, urlStr string, fs filesys.Filesys) (*DoltDB, error) {
	return LoadDoltDBWithParams(ctx, nbf, urlStr, fs, nil)
}

func LoadDoltDBWithParams(ctx context.Context, nbf *types.NomsBinFormat, urlStr string, fs filesys.Filesys, params map[string]interface{}) (*DoltDB, error) {
	if params == nil {
		params = make(map[string]any)
	}

	if urlStr == LocalDirDoltDB {
		exists, isDir := fs.Exists(dbfactory.DoltDataDir)
		if !exists {
			return nil, ErrMissingDoltDataDir
		} else if !isDir {
			return nil, errors.New("file exists where the dolt data directory should be")
		}

		absPath, err := fs.Abs(dbfactory.DoltDataDir)
=== AGENT.md ===
# AGENT.md - Dolt Database Operations Guide

This file provides guidance for AI agents working with Dolt databases to maximize productivity and follow best practices.

## Quick Start

Dolt is "Git for Data" - a SQL database with version control capabilities. All Git commands have Dolt equivalents:
- `git add` → `dolt add`  
- `git commit` → `dolt commit`
- `git branch` → `dolt branch`
- `git merge` → `dolt merge`
- `git diff` → `dolt diff`

For help and documentation on commands, you can run `dolt --help` and `dolt <command> --help`.

## Essential Dolt CLI Commands

### Repository Operations
```bash
# Initialize new database
dolt init

# Clone existing database
dolt clone <remote-url>

# Show current status
dolt status

# View commit history
dolt log
```

### Branch Management
```bash
# List branches
dolt branch

# Create new branch
dolt branch <branch-name>

# Switch branches
dolt checkout <branch-name>

# Create and switch to new branch
dolt checkout -b <branch-name>
```

### Checkout Behavior with Running SQL Servers
- `dolt checkout` on the CLI only affects the shell process that runs the command. When a `dolt sql-server` is running, existing SQL connections keep their current branch until they explicitly switch.
- Each SQL session (CLI `dolt sql`, MySQL client, application connection) maintains its own active branch. Run `CALL dolt_checkout('<branch>');` at the beginning of every session or scripted block to ensure you are on the correct branch.
- Chain branch changes inside scripts: start with `CALL dolt_checkout('<branch>');`, then run your queries. Do not assume a previous checkout persists for new connections.
- When automating, include the checkout in the same transaction / session context where the data changes execute.
- A good way to make sure a `dolt sql` session connects to the br1 branch for instance is `dolt --branch br1 sql`.

### Data Operations
```bash
# Stage changes
dolt add <table-name>
dolt add .  # stage all changes

# Commit changes
dolt commit -m "commit message"

# View differences
dolt diff
dolt diff <table-name>
dolt diff <branch1> <branch2>

# Merge branches
dolt merge <branch-name>
```

## Starting and Connecting to Dolt SQL Server

### Start SQL Server
```bash
# Start server on default port (3306)
dolt sql-server

# Start on specific port
dolt sql-server --port=3307

# Start with specific host
dolt sql-server --host=0.0.0.0 --port=3307

# Start in background
dolt sql-server --port=3307 &
```

### Connecting to SQL Server
```bash
# Connect with dolt sql command
dolt sql

# Connect with mysql client
mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -P 3306 -u root

# Connect with specific database
mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -P 3306 -u root -D <database-name>
```

## Dolt Testing with dolt_test System Table

### Unit Testing with dolt_test

The dolt_test system table provides a powerful way to create and run unit tests for your database. This is the preferred method for testing data integrity, business rules, and schema validation.

#### Creating Tests

Tests are created by inserting rows into the `dolt_tests` system table:

```sql
-- Create a simple test
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_user_count', 
    'validation', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) as user_count FROM users;', 
    'row_count',
    '>',
    '0'
);

-- Create a test with expected result
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_valid_emails', 
    'validation', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE email NOT LIKE "%@%";', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);

-- Create a schema validation test
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_users_schema', 
    'schema', 
    'DESCRIBE users;', 
    'row_count',
    '>=',
    '5'
);
```

#### Test Structure

Each test row contains:
- test_name: Unique identifier for the test
- test_group: Optional grouping for tests (e.g., 'validation', 'schema', 'integration')
- test_query: SQL query to execute
- assertion_type: Type of assertion ('expected_rows', 'expected_columns', 'expected_single_value')
- assertion_comparator: Comparison operator ('==', '>', '<', '>=', '<=', '!=')
- assertion_value: Expected value for comparison

#### Running Tests

```sql
-- Run all tests
SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run();

-- Run specific test
SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run('test_user_count');

-- Run tests with filtering
SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run() WHERE test_name LIKE 'test_user%' AND status != 'PASS';
```

#### Test Result Interpretation

The dolt_test_run() function returns:
- test_name: Name of the test
- status: PASS, FAIL, or ERROR
- actual_result: Actual query result
- expected_result: Expected result
- message: Additional details

#### Advanced Testing Examples

```sql
-- Test data integrity
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_no_orphaned_orders', 
    'integrity', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o LEFT JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id WHERE u.id IS NULL;', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);

-- Test business rules
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_positive_prices', 
    'business_rules', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE price <= 0;', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);

-- Test complex relationships
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_order_totals', 
    'integrity', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY o.id HAVING SUM(oi.quantity * oi.price) != o.total;', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);
```

### Dolt CI for DoltHub Integration

Dolt CI is specifically designed for running tests on DoltHub when pull requests are created. Use this only for tests you want to run automatically on DoltHub.

#### Prerequisites for DoltHub CI
- Requires Dolt v1.43.14 or later
- Must initialize CI capabilities: `dolt ci init`
- Workflows defined in YAML files

#### Available CI Commands
```bash
# Initialize CI capabilities
dolt ci init

# List available workflows
dolt ci ls

# View workflow details
dolt ci view <workflow-name>

# View specific job in workflow
dolt ci view <workflow-name> <job-name>

# Run workflow locally (for testing before DoltHub)
dolt ci run <workflow-name>
```

#### Creating CI Workflows for DoltHub

Create workflow files that will run on DoltHub when pull requests are opened:

```yaml
name: doltHub validation workflow
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - master
      - main
jobs:
  - name: validate schema
    steps:
      - name: check required tables exist
        saved_query_name: show_tables
        expected_rows: ">= 3"
      
      - name: validate user data
        saved_query_name: user_count_check
        expected_columns: "== 1"
        expected_rows: "> 0"
  
  - name: data integrity checks
    steps:
      - name: check email format
        saved_query_name: valid_emails
        expected_rows: "== 0"  # No invalid emails
```

### Best Practices for Testing

1. **Use dolt_test for Unit Testing**
   - Create tests for data validation
   - Test business rules and constraints
   - Validate schema changes
   - Run tests frequently during development

2. **Use Dolt CI for DoltHub Integration**
   - Only for tests that should run on pull requests
   - Focus on integration and deployment validation
   - Test against production-like data

3. **Create Comprehensive Test Suites**
   - Test data integrity constraints
   - Validate business rules
   - Check schema requirements
   - Verify data relationships

4. **Version Control Your Tests**
   - Commit test definitions to repository
   - Track changes to test configuration
   - Use branches for test development

## System Tables for Version Control

Dolt exposes version control operations through system tables accessible via SQL:

### Core System Tables
```sql
-- View commit history
SELECT * FROM dolt_log;

-- Check current status
SELECT * FROM dolt_status;

-- View branch information
SELECT * FROM dolt_branches;

-- See table diffs
SELECT * FROM dolt_diff_<table_name>;

-- View schema changes
SELECT * FROM dolt_schema_diff;

-- Check conflicts during merge
SELECT * FROM dolt_conflicts_<table_name>;

-- View commit metadata
SELECT * FROM dolt_commits;
```

### Version Control Operations via SQL

When working in SQL sessions, you can execute version control operations using stored procedures:

```sql
-- Stage and commit changes
CALL dolt_add('.');
CALL dolt_commit('-m', 'commit message');

-- Branch operations
CALL dolt_branch('<branch_name>');
CALL dolt_checkout('<branch_name>');
CALL dolt_merge('<branch_name>');
```

**Note:** Use CLI commands (`dolt add`, `dolt commit`, etc.) for most operations. SQL procedures are useful when already in a SQL session.

### Advanced System Tables
```sql
-- View remotes
SELECT * FROM dolt_remotes;

-- Check merge conflicts
SELECT * FROM dolt_conflicts;

-- View statistics
SELECT * FROM dolt_statistics;

-- See ignored tables
SELECT * FROM dolt_ignore;
```

## CLI vs SQL Approach

**Prefer CLI commands for:**
- Version control operations (add, commit, branch, merge)
- Repository management (init, clone, push, pull)
- Conflict resolution
- Status checking and history viewing

**Use SQL for:**
- Data queries and analysis
- Complex data transformations
- Examining system tables (dolt_log, dolt_status, etc.)
- When already in an active SQL session

## Schema Design Recommendations

### Use UUID Keys Instead of Auto-Increment

For Dolt's version control features, use UUID primary keys instead of auto-increment:

```sql
-- Recommended
CREATE TABLE users (
    id varchar(36) default(uuid()) primary key,
    name varchar(255)
);

-- Avoid auto-increment with Dolt
-- id int auto_increment primary key
```

**Benefits:**
- Prevents merge conflicts across branches and database clones
- Automatic generation with default(uuid())
- Works seamlessly in distributed environments

## Best Practices for Agents

### 1. Always Work on Feature Branches
```bash
# Create feature branch before making changes
dolt checkout -b feature/agent-changes

# Make changes on feature branch
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'Alice');"

# Stage and commit
dolt add .
dolt commit -m "Add new user Alice"

# Switch back to main to merge
dolt checkout main
dolt merge feature/agent-changes
```

### 2. Use SQL for Data Operations, CLI for Version Control
```bash
# Use dolt sql for data changes
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'Alice');"
dolt sql -q "UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1 WHERE category = 'electronics';"

# Check status and commit using CLI
dolt status
dolt add .
dolt commit -m "Update user and product data"
```

### 3. Validate Changes with System Tables
```sql
-- Before major operations, check current state
SELECT * FROM dolt_status;
SELECT * FROM dolt_branches;

-- After changes, verify with diffs
SELECT * FROM dolt_diff_users;
SELECT * FROM dolt_schema_diff;
```

### 4. Use dolt_test for Data Validation
Create tests to validate:
- Data integrity after changes
- Schema compatibility
- Business rule compliance
- Cross-table relationships

### 5. Handle Conflicts Gracefully
```bash
# Check for conflicts using CLI
dolt conflicts cat <table_name>
dolt conflicts resolve --ours <table_name>
dolt conflicts resolve --theirs <table_name>

# Or use SQL to examine conflicts
dolt sql -q "SELECT * FROM dolt_conflicts_<table_name>;"
```

## Common Workflow Examples

### Data Migration Workflow
```bash
# Create migration branch
dolt checkout -b migration/update-schema

# Apply schema changes via SQL
dolt sql -q "ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email VARCHAR(255);"

# Create validation tests
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES ('test_users_schema', 'schema', 'DESCRIBE users;', 'row_count', '>=', '6');"
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES ('test_email_column', 'schema', 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE email IS NULL;', 'row_count', '>=', '0');"

# Run tests to validate changes
dolt sql -q "SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run();"

# Stage and commit
dolt add .
dolt commit -m "Add email column to users table"

# Merge back
dolt checkout main
dolt merge migration/update-schema
```

### Data Analysis Workflow
```bash
# Create analysis branch
dolt checkout -b analysis/user-behavior

# Create analysis tables via SQL
dolt sql -q "CREATE TABLE user_metrics AS 
            SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) as actions 
            FROM user_actions 
            GROUP BY user_id;"

# Create tests to validate analysis
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES ('test_metrics_created', 'analysis', 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_metrics;', 'row_count', '>', '0');"
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES ('test_metrics_integrity', 'integrity', 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM user_metrics um LEFT JOIN users u ON um.user_id = u.id WHERE u.id IS NULL;', 'row_count', '==', '0');"

# Run tests to validate analysis
dolt sql -q "SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run();"

# Stage and commit using CLI
dolt add user_metrics
dolt commit -m "Add user behavior analysis"
```

## Integration with External Tools

### Database Clients
Most MySQL clients work with Dolt:
- MySQL Workbench
- phpMyAdmin  
- DataGrip
- DBeaver

### Backup and Sync
```bash
# Push to remote
dolt push origin main

# Pull changes
dolt pull origin main

# Clone for backup
dolt clone <remote-url> backup-location
```

type SimpleTableResolver struct{}

var _ TableResolver = SimpleTableResolver{}

func (t SimpleTableResolver) ResolveTableInsensitive(ctx *sql.Context, root RootValue, tblName TableName) (trueTableName TableName, table *Table, exists bool, err error) {
	trueTableNameString, exists, err := root.ResolveTableName(ctx, tblName)
	if err != nil || !exists {
		return TableName{}, nil, false, err
	}
	trueTableName = TableName{
		Name:   trueTableNameString,
		Schema: tblName.Schema,
	}
	table, exists, err = root.GetTable(ctx, trueTableName)
	return trueTableName, table, exists, err
}

func (t SimpleTableResolver) ResolveTable(ctx *sql.Context, root RootValue, tblName TableName) (table *Table, exists bool, err error) {
	return root.GetTable(ctx, tblName)
}

// DoltDB wraps access to the underlying noms database and hides some of the details of the underlying storage.
type DoltDB struct {
	db  hooksDatabase
	vrw types.ValueReadWriter
	ns  tree.NodeStore

	// databaseName holds the name of the database for this DoltDB instance. Note that this name may not be
	// populated for all DoltDB instances. For filesystem based databases, the database name is determined
	// by looking through the filepath in reverse, finding the first .dolt directory, and then taking the
	// parent directory as the database name. For non-filesystem based databases, the database name will not
	// currently be populated.
	databaseName string

	// Keep a LRU Cache of materialized commits to speed up future commit resolutions
	commitCache *lru.Cache[hash.Hash, *OptionalCommit]
}

// IsWorkingSetRef reports whether |ref| identifies the working set or staging area rather than a commit.
func IsWorkingSetRef(ref string) bool {
	return ref == Working || ref == Staged
}

// DoltDBFromCS creates a DoltDB from a noms chunks.ChunkStore
func DoltDBFromCS(cs chunks.ChunkStore, databaseName string) (*DoltDB, error) {
	vrw := types.NewValueStore(cs)
	ns := tree.NewNodeStore(cs)
	db := datas.NewTypesDatabase(vrw, ns)
	commitCache, err := lru.New[hash.Hash, *OptionalCommit](commitCacheSize)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, err
	}
	ret := &DoltDB{
		db:           hooksDatabase{Database: db},
		vrw:          vrw,
		ns:           ns,
		databaseName: databaseName,
		commitCache:  commitCache,
	}
	ret.db.db = ret
	return ret, nil
}

// GetDatabaseName returns the name of the database.
// Note: This can return an empty string if the database name is not populated.
func (ddb *DoltDB) GetDatabaseName() string {
	return ddb.databaseName
}

// ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB unwraps a DoltDB to a datas.Database.
// This method should really only be used in cases where we need to get to the internals of the database, such
// as scanning for corruption or migrating data formats. Generally avoid this function.
func ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB(ddb *DoltDB) datas.Database {
	return ddb.db
}

// LoadDoltDB will acquire a reference to the underlying noms db.  If the Location is InMemDoltDB then a reference

func LoadDoltDB(ctx context.Context, nbf *types.NomsBinFormat, urlStr string, fs filesys.Filesys) (*DoltDB, error) {
	return LoadDoltDBWithParams(ctx, nbf, urlStr, fs, nil)
}

=== AGENT.md ===
# AGENT.md - Dolt Database Operations Guide

This file provides guidance for AI agents working with Dolt databases to maximize productivity and follow best practices.

## Quick Start

Dolt is "Git for Data" - a SQL database with version control capabilities. All Git commands have Dolt equivalents:
- `git add` → `dolt add`  
- `git commit` → `dolt commit`
- `git branch` → `dolt branch`
- `git merge` → `dolt merge`
- `git diff` → `dolt diff`

For help and documentation on commands, you can run `dolt --help` and `dolt <command> --help`.

## Essential Dolt CLI Commands

### Repository Operations
```bash
# Initialize new database
dolt init

# Clone existing database
dolt clone <remote-url>

# Show current status
dolt status

# View commit history
dolt log
```

### Branch Management
```bash
# List branches
dolt branch

# Create new branch
dolt branch <branch-name>

# Switch branches
dolt checkout <branch-name>

# Create and switch to new branch
dolt checkout -b <branch-name>
```

### Checkout Behavior with Running SQL Servers
- `dolt checkout` on the CLI only affects the shell process that runs the command. When a `dolt sql-server` is running, existing SQL connections keep their current branch until they explicitly switch.
- Each SQL session (CLI `dolt sql`, MySQL client, application connection) maintains its own active branch. Run `CALL dolt_checkout('<branch>');` at the beginning of every session or scripted block to ensure you are on the correct branch.
- Chain branch changes inside scripts: start with `CALL dolt_checkout('<branch>');`, then run your queries. Do not assume a previous checkout persists for new connections.
- When automating, include the checkout in the same transaction / session context where the data changes execute.
- A good way to make sure a `dolt sql` session connects to the br1 branch for instance is `dolt --branch br1 sql`.

### Data Operations
```bash
# Stage changes
dolt add <table-name>
dolt add .  # stage all changes

# Commit changes
dolt commit -m "commit message"

# View differences
dolt diff
dolt diff <table-name>
dolt diff <branch1> <branch2>

# Merge branches
dolt merge <branch-name>
```

## Starting and Connecting to Dolt SQL Server

### Start SQL Server
```bash
# Start server on default port (3306)
dolt sql-server

# Start on specific port
dolt sql-server --port=3307

# Start with specific host
dolt sql-server --host=0.0.0.0 --port=3307

# Start in background
dolt sql-server --port=3307 &
```

### Connecting to SQL Server
```bash
# Connect with dolt sql command
dolt sql

# Connect with mysql client
mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -P 3306 -u root

# Connect with specific database
mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -P 3306 -u root -D <database-name>
```

## Dolt Testing with dolt_test System Table

### Unit Testing with dolt_test

The dolt_test system table provides a powerful way to create and run unit tests for your database. This is the preferred method for testing data integrity, business rules, and schema validation.

#### Creating Tests

Tests are created by inserting rows into the `dolt_tests` system table:

```sql
-- Create a simple test
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_user_count', 
    'validation', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) as user_count FROM users;', 
    'row_count',
    '>',
    '0'
);

-- Create a test with expected result
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_valid_emails', 
    'validation', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE email NOT LIKE "%@%";', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);

-- Create a schema validation test
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_users_schema', 
    'schema', 
    'DESCRIBE users;', 
    'row_count',
    '>=',
    '5'
);
```

#### Test Structure

Each test row contains:
- test_name: Unique identifier for the test
- test_group: Optional grouping for tests (e.g., 'validation', 'schema', 'integration')
- test_query: SQL query to execute
- assertion_type: Type of assertion ('expected_rows', 'expected_columns', 'expected_single_value')
- assertion_comparator: Comparison operator ('==', '>', '<', '>=', '<=', '!=')
- assertion_value: Expected value for comparison

#### Running Tests

```sql
-- Run all tests
SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run();

-- Run specific test
SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run('test_user_count');

-- Run tests with filtering
SELECT * FROM dolt_test_run() WHERE test_name LIKE 'test_user%' AND status != 'PASS';
```

#### Test Result Interpretation

The dolt_test_run() function returns:
- test_name: Name of the test
- status: PASS, FAIL, or ERROR
- actual_result: Actual query result
- expected_result: Expected result
- message: Additional details

#### Advanced Testing Examples

```sql
-- Test data integrity
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_no_orphaned_orders', 
    'integrity', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o LEFT JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id WHERE u.id IS NULL;', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);

-- Test business rules
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_positive_prices', 
    'business_rules', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE price <= 0;', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);

-- Test complex relationships
INSERT INTO `dolt_tests` VALUES (
    'test_order_totals', 
    'integrity', 
    'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id GROUP BY o.id HAVING SUM(oi.quantity * oi.price) != o.total;', 
    'row_count',
    '==',
    '0'
);
```

### Dolt CI for DoltHub Integration

Dolt CI is specifically designed for running tests on DoltHub when pull requests are created. Use this only for tests you want to run automatically on DoltHub.

#### Prerequisites for DoltHub CI
- Requires Dolt v1.43.14 or later
- Must initialize CI capabilities: `dolt ci init`
- Workflows defined in YAML files

#### Available CI Commands
```bash
# Initialize CI capabilities
dolt ci init

# List available workflows
dolt ci ls

# View workflow details
dolt ci view <workflow-name>

# View specific job in workflow
dolt ci view <workflow-name> <job-name>

# Run workflow locally (for testing before DoltHub)
dolt ci run <workflow-name>
```

#### Creating CI Workflows for DoltHub

Create workflow files that will run on DoltHub when pull requests are opened:

```yaml
name: doltHub validation workflow
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - master
      - main
jobs:
  - name: validate schema
    steps:
      - name: check required tables exist
        saved_query_name: show_tables
        expected_rows: ">= 3"
      
      - name: validate user data
        saved_query_name: user_count_check
        expected_columns: "== 1"
        expected_rows: "> 0"
  
  - name: data integrity checks
    steps:
      - name: check email format
        saved_query_name: valid_emails
        expected_rows: "== 0"  # No invalid emails
```

### Best Practices for Testing

1. **Use dolt_test for Unit Testing**
   - Create tests for data validation
   - Test business rules and constraints
   - Validate schema changes
   - Run tests frequently during development

2. **Use Dolt CI for DoltHub Integration**
   - Only for tests that should run on pull requests
   - Focus on integration and deployment validation
   - Test against production-like data

3. **Create Comprehensive Test Suites**
   - Test data integrity constraints
   - Validate business rules
   - Check schema requirements
   - Verify data relationships

4. **Version Control Your Tests**
   - Commit test definitions to repository
   - Track changes to test configuration
   - Use branches for test development

## System Tables for Version Control

Dolt exposes version control operations through system tables accessible via SQL:

### Core System Tables
```sql
-- View commit history
SELECT * FROM dolt_log;

-- Check current status
SELECT * FROM dolt_status;

-- View branch information
SELECT * FROM dolt_branches;

-- See table diffs
SELECT * FROM dolt_diff_<table_name>;

-- View schema changes
SELECT * FROM dolt_schema_diff;

-- Check conflicts during merge
SELECT * FROM dolt_conflicts_<table_name>;

-- View commit metadata
SELECT * FROM dolt_commits;
```

### Version Control Operations via SQL

When working in SQL sessions, you can execute version control operations using stored procedures:

```sql
-- Stage and commit changes
CALL dolt_add('.');
CALL dolt_commit('-m', 'commit message');

-- Branch operations
CALL dolt_branch('<branch_name>');
CALL dolt_checkout('<branch_name>');
CALL dolt_merge('<branch_name>');
```

**Note:** Use CLI commands (`dolt add`, `dolt commit`, etc.) for most operations. SQL procedures are useful when already in a SQL session.

### Advanced System Tables
```sql
-- View remotes
SELECT * FROM dolt_remotes;

-- Check merge conflicts
SELECT * FROM dolt_conflicts;

-- View statistics
SELECT * FROM dolt_statistics;

-- See ignored tables
SELECT * FROM dolt_ignore;
```

## CLI vs SQL Approach

**Prefer CLI commands for:**
- Version control operations (add, commit, branch, merge)
- Repository management (init, clone, push, pull)
- Conflict resolution
- Status checking and history viewing

**Use SQL for:**
- Data queries and analysis
- Complex data transformations
- Examining system tables (dolt_log, dolt_status, etc.)
- When already in an active SQL session

## Schema Design Recommendations

### Use UUID Keys Instead of Auto-Increment

For Dolt's version control features, use UUID primary keys instead of auto-increment:

```sql
-- Recommended
CREATE TABLE users (
    id varchar(36) default(uuid()) primary key,
    name varchar(255)
);

-- Avoid auto-increment with Dolt
-- id int auto_increment primary key
```

**Benefits:**
- Prevents merge conflicts across branches and database clones
- Automatic generation with default(uuid())
- Works seamlessly in distributed environments

## Best Practices for Agents

### 1. Always Work on Feature Branches
```bash
# Create feature branch before making changes
dolt checkout -b feature/agent-changes

# Make changes on feature branch
dolt sql -q "INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'Alice');"

# Stage and commit
dolt add .
dolt commit -m "Add new user Alice"

# Switch back to main to merge
dolt checkout main
dolt merge feature/agent-changes
```

74:type ChunkStore interface {
75-	// Get the Chunk for the value of the hash in the store. If the hash is
76-	// absent from the store EmptyChunk is returned.
77-	Get(ctx context.Context, h hash.Hash) (Chunk, error)
78-
79-	// GetMany gets the Chunks with |hashes| from the store. On return,
80-	// |foundChunks| will have been fully sent all chunks which have been
81-	// found. Any non-present chunks will silently be ignored.
82-	GetMany(ctx context.Context, hashes hash.HashSet, found func(context.Context, *Chunk)) error
83-
84-	// Returns true iff the value at the address |h| is contained in the
85-	// store
86-	Has(ctx context.Context, h hash.Hash) (bool, error)
87-
88-	// Returns a new HashSet containing any members of |hashes| that are
89-	// absent from the store.
90-	HasMany(ctx context.Context, hashes hash.HashSet) (absent hash.HashSet, err error)
91-
92-	// Put caches c in the ChunkSource. Upon return, c must be visible to
93-	// subsequent Get and Has calls, but must not be persistent until a call
94-	// to Flush(). Put may be called concurrently with other calls to Put(),
95-	// Get(), GetMany(), Has() and HasMany(). Will return an error if the
96-	// addrs returned by `getAddrs` are absent from the chunk store.
97-	Put(ctx context.Context, c Chunk, getAddrs InsertAddrsCurry) error
98-
99-	// Returns the NomsBinFormat with which this ChunkSource is compatible.
100-	Version() string
101-
102-	// Returns the current access mode for this opened ChunkStore.
103-	AccessMode() ExclusiveAccessMode
104-
105-	// Rebase brings this ChunkStore into sync with the persistent storage's
106-	// current root.
107-	Rebase(ctx context.Context) error
108-
109-	// Root returns the root of the database as of the time the ChunkStore
110-	// was opened or the most recent call to Rebase.
111-	Root(ctx context.Context) (hash.Hash, error)
112-
113-	// Commit atomically attempts to persist all novel Chunks and update the
114-	// persisted root hash from last to current (or keeps it the same).

106-	// UndropDatabase attempts to restore the database |dbName| that was previously dropped.
107-	// The restored database will appear identically when accessed through the SQL
108-	// interface, but may be stored in a slightly different location on disk
109-	// (e.g. a root database will be restored as a regular/non-root database,
110-	// databases original stored with hyphens in their directory name will be rewritten
111-	// to underscores to match their SQL database name).

content.text:
I have the map. Here's the orientation.

## What this project is

Dolt — "Git for Data": a MySQL-compatible SQL database with Git-style version control (branch/merge/commit/push/pull) over *tables*. Single Go module at `go/go.mod`; the CLI, the SQL server, and the storage engine all live in it. Sibling dirs (`integration-tests/`, `proto/`, `docker/`) are not production Go code for our purposes.

## Layout (top-down)

| Layer | Path | Role |
|---|---|---|
| CLI entry + commands | `go/cmd/dolt/` (`dolt.go`, `commands/`, `doltcmd/`) | every `dolt <cmd>`; obtains a `*env.DoltEnv` and works through it |
| Env / repo state | `go/libraries/doltcore/env/` | `DoltEnv` (`environment.go:82`), `DbData` (`repo_state.go:60`), remotes (`remotes.go:194`), plus `env/actions/` (commit, checkout, reset, remotes, commitwalk) |
| SQL engine integration | `go/libraries/doltcore/sqle/` | `Database` (`database.go:80`), `DatabaseProvider` (`database_provider.go:99`), sessions (`dsess/`), system tables (`dtables/`), procedures (`dprocedures/`), replication/cluster (`commit_hooks.go`, `cluster/`), `auto_gc.go`, `remotesrv.go` |
| **Core database object** | `go/libraries/doltcore/doltdb/` | `DoltDB` — this is the rework target |
| Value/index layer | `doltdb/durable/`, `go/store/prolly/`, `go/store/val/`, `go/store/types/`, `go/store/types/…/tree` | root value → table → prolly-tree indexes |
| Noms dataset layer | `go/store/datas/` | `Database`, `Dataset`, commits, working sets, tags, stash |
| Chunk storage | `go/store/chunks/` (interface, `chunk_store.go:74`), `go/store/nbs/` (NomsBlockStore, journal, manifest), `go/store/blobstore/` | root hash tracking + chunk persistence |
| Remote storage | `go/libraries/doltcore/remotestorage/`, `remotesrv/`, `dbfactory/` | remote chunk stores; `dbfactory` is the open/URL-scheme factory |

## The object you're reworking

**`doltdb.DoltDB` — `go/libraries/doltcore/doltdb/doltdb.go:126`.** Only 4 fields:

```go
db  hooksDatabase        // datas.Database + commit hooks (hooksdatabase.go)
vrw types.ValueReadWriter
ns  tree.NodeStore
databaseName string
commitCache  *lru.Cache[hash.Hash, *OptionalCommit]
```

Construction / lifecycle:
- `DoltDBFromCS(cs chunks.ChunkStore, name)` — `doltdb.go:148`; wraps a ChunkStore into ValueStore + NodeStore + `datas.Database`, self-referencing via `ret.db.db = ret`.
- `LoadDoltDB` / `LoadDoltDBWithParams` — `doltdb.go:182`+, goes through `dbfactory` (URL string + params, e.g. `dbfactory.ChunkJournalParam` at `doltdb.go:207`).
- `Close()` `:352`, `Teardown()` `:356`.

**How storage is reached today (the leak surface):** every chunk-level operation re-derives the store on the fly via `datas.ChunkStoreFromDatabase(ddb.db)` — there is no retained `cs` field. Call sites: `NomsRoot :239/240`, `AccessMode :243`, `CommitRoot :248`, `Has :252`, `Teardown :357`, `Rebase :1953`, `GC :1967`, pull/push `:2074-2118`, table-file store checks `:2124-2179`. Root swap bottoms out at `nbs.NomsBlockStore.Root` (`go/store/nbs/store.go:1548`) and `.Commit(ctx, current, last)` (`:1558`). GC safepoints live in `doltdb/gcctx/` (`gc_safepoint_controller.go`).

**Root tracking above the chunk store:** `RootValue` (`doltdb/root_val.go`, storage in `root_val_storage.go`), `WorkingSet` (`workingset.go`), `Commit` (`commit.go`), all read/written through `ReadRootValue :896` / `WriteRootValue :799` and ref mutators `SetHead :1028`, `FastForward :960`, `CommitWithParent* :1041/:1059`.

## Who retains it (the audit's starting set)

Named `*doltdb.DoltDB` fields, non-test — `env/environment.go:92` (`DoltEnv.doltDB`, lazily loaded under `loadDBOnce`), `env/repo_state.go:60` (`DbData.Ddb` — the struct handed to nearly every command), `env/remotes.go:194-195`, `sqle/database.go:80`, `sqle/database_provider.go:99` (`gitRemotes` map), `sqle/read_replica_database.go:40`, `sqle/auto_gc.go:136,302`, `sqle/remotesrv.go:88`, `sqle/commit_hooks.go:38,102,111`, `sqle/cluster/commithook.go:50,52`, `sqle/dsess/transactions.go:93`, `sqle/dprocedures/dolt_gc.go:78,172`, and ~a dozen `dtables/*.go` system-table structs.

**The holders that don't name the type** — the ones the task warns about — reach it through interfaces instead: `dsess.SqlDatabase.DoltDatabases() []*doltdb.DoltDB` (`dsess/session_db_provider.go:132-149`) and `DbData()` at `:147`; `sqle/database.go:199` and `read_replica_database.go:113` implement it. Anything stored as `dsess.SqlDatabase`, `sql.Database`, `datas.Database`, `chunks.ChunkStore`, or a closure capturing `ddb` is a retainer that greps for `*doltdb.DoltDB` will miss. Session-side state lives in `dsess/database_session_state.go` and `dsess/session.go`; that's where Task 2+ should start pulling.

// NomsRoot returns the hash of the noms dataset map
func (ddb *DoltDB) NomsRoot(ctx context.Context) (hash.Hash, error) {
	return datas.ChunkStoreFromDatabase(ddb.db).Root(ctx)
}

func (ddb *DoltDB) AccessMode() chunks.ExclusiveAccessMode {
	return datas.ChunkStoreFromDatabase(ddb.db).AccessMode()
}

// CommitRoot executes a chunkStore commit, atomically swapping the root hash of the database manifest
func (ddb *DoltDB) CommitRoot(ctx context.Context, current, last hash.Hash) (bool, error) {
	return datas.ChunkStoreFromDatabase(ddb.db).Commit(ctx, current, last)
}

func (ddb *DoltDB) Has(ctx context.Context, h hash.Hash) (bool, error) {
	return datas.ChunkStoreFromDatabase(ddb.db).Has(ctx, h)
}

func (ddb *DoltDB) CSMetricsSummary() string {
	return datas.GetCSStatSummaryForDB(ddb.db)
}

content.text:
Here's the current contract, layer by layer.

## A. The storage root: what it is and who owns it

There are **two distinct "roots"** in Dolt and conflating them is the main hazard in this rework:

1. **Noms root / manifest root** — one `hash.Hash` per database that addresses the *dataset map* (branch → commit, working-set refs, tags, stash, stats). This is the thing that gets atomically swapped.
2. **`RootValue`** — the per-branch table map (`doltdb/root_val.go:531 GetTable`, `root_val_storage.go`). It is an ordinary immutable value written into the chunk store; it is *not* the storage root. It only becomes visible when a dataset update moves the noms root.

### Root ownership chain (bottom-up)

| Layer | file:line | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Persisted manifest | `store/nbs/store.go:1608 updateManifest` | the durable root; CAS `if nbs.upstream.root != last → errLastRootMismatch` |
| `NomsBlockStore.Root` | `store/nbs/store.go:1548` | in-memory cached root under `nbs.mu.RLock`, valid as of open or last `Rebase` |
| `NomsBlockStore.Commit` | `store/nbs/store.go:1558` → `.commit :1566` | CAS swap `(current, last)`; retries on `errOptimisticLockFailedTables`, returns `false` on root move. Note the GC hook at `:1573` (`keeperFunc` → `waitForGC`) and the no-op fast path at `:1583` that degrades to a `rebase` |
| `chunks.ChunkStore` iface | `store/chunks/chunk_store.go:74` — `Root :111`, `Commit :114`, `Rebase :107` | the narrow contract everything above codes to |
| `types.ValueStore` | `store/types/value_store.go:375 Root`, `:542 Commit`, `:383 Rebase`, `:188 ChunkStore()` | owns `cs`, gates `Commit` behind `waitForNotFinalizingGC` |
| `datas.rootTracker` | `store/datas/database_common.go:51` | deliberately narrowed view — *only* `Root` + `Commit`; `var _ rootTracker = &types.ValueStore{}` at `:67` |
| `datas.database` | `database_common.go:37` (`ValueStore` embedded + `rt` + `ns`) | dataset-map layer |

### The swap loop — the actual contract

`store/datas/database_common.go:815 (*database).update` is the single writer of the noms root:

```
for {
  root      := db.rt.Root(ctx)                 // :825
  datasets  := db.loadDatasetsRefmap(ctx, root) // :831
  datasets  := editFB(ctx, datasets)            // caller's mutation
  newRoot   := db.WriteValue(storeroot_flatbuffer(datasets)).TargetHash() // :843-848
  err       := db.tryCommitChunks(ctx, newRoot, root)  // :850
  if err != ErrOptimisticLockFailed { return err }      // else retry
}
```

`tryCommitChunks` (`:946`) is the thin adapter: `rt.Commit(new, current)` → `false` becomes `ErrOptimisticLockFailed` (`:44`). **Every** ref mutation funnels here — `doCommit :550`, `doSetHead :206`, `doFastForward :346`, `doDelete :856`, tag/stash/stats writes — via `doHeadUpdate :966`, which re-reads the dataset afterward.

### Above that: DoltDB's root surface

`doltdb/doltdb.go` exposes the raw root to callers who then do their *own* CAS outside `update`:

- `NomsRoot(ctx) :239` — read
- `CommitRoot(ctx, current, last) :248` — "atomically swapping the root hash of the database manifest" (its own comment)
- `Rebase(ctx) :1953` — reload manifest, for out-of-process writers
- `ResolveWorkingSetAtRoot :769`, `ResolveByNomsRoot :604`, `GetHashForRefStrByNomsRoot :375`, `DatasetsByRootHash`, `VisitRefsOfTypeByNomsRoot :1458`, `GetBranchesByNomsRoot :1224` — **the whole "read as of a pinned root" family**. This is the snapshot-isolation mechanism and it is the part most likely to break under a rework: it is not a handle, just a hash passed down per call.

The `hooksDatabase` wrapper (`doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:26`) sits between `DoltDB` and `datas.Database`, holding a back-pointer `db *DoltDB` and `hooks []CommitHook`, firing post-swap replication/auto-GC hooks. `DoltDBFromCS` closes the cycle at `doltdb.go:163`: `ret.db.db = ret`.

## B. How chunk storage is reached from DoltDB — the leak surface

**`DoltDB` retains no `ChunkStore` field.** Every chunk-level op re-derives it inline:

```go
datas.ChunkStoreFromDatabase(ddb.db)   // store/datas/database.go:221 → db.chunkStore()
                                       // database_common.go:70 → db.ValueStore.ChunkStore()
                                       // value_store.go:188 → lvs.cs
```

Call sites in `doltdb.go`: `:240` (NomsRoot), `:244` (AccessMode), `:249` (CommitRoot), `:253` (Has), `:357` (Teardown), `:945` (push concurrency control), `:1156` (CommitDangling), `:1954` (Rebase), `:2074-2075` and `:2117-2118` (pull/push src+dest), `:2126` (`chunks.TableFileStore` type assertion), `:2134/:2144/:2159/:2179` (table-file ops). Plus `CSMetricsSummary :256` via `datas.GetCSStatSummaryForDB`.

Consequences of the current contract:
- The store is reached through **three hops of unexported plumbing** (`Database.chunkStore()` is unexported; `ChunkStoreFromDatabase` is the only door), so a rework can change the interior — but `ChunkStoreFromDatabase` is exported and callable on any `datas.Database`, so external holders can bypass `DoltDB` entirely.
- `ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB` (`doltdb.go:172` region, comment says "Generally avoid this function") hands out the raw `datas.Database` — combined with the above, that is a full escape hatch to the ChunkStore.
- The `chunks.TableFileStore` assertion at `:2126` means the *dynamic* type of the store is load-bearing; any wrapper/decorator inserted by the rework breaks table-file push/pull and clone silently unless it forwards the assertion.

## C. Query → bytes: the full stack

| # | Layer | file:line | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SQL session | `sqle/dsess/session.go:348 StartTransaction`, `:446 CommitTransaction` | per-connection entry; pins a snapshot |
| 2 | Transaction snapshot | `sqle/dsess/transactions.go:86 DoltTransaction` / `:92 dbRoot{db, dbName, rootHash}` | **the noms root is captured per-DB at tx start** (`NewDoltTransaction :102`, `db.DbData().Ddb.NomsRoot(ctx)` at `:110`) — this is Dolt's MVCC |
| 3 | Branch session state | `sqle/dsess/database_session_state.go:102 branchState` | holds `workingSet`, `headCommit`, `headRoot`, `writeSession`, `dbData` per branch |
| 4 | Session root accessors | `session.go:992 GetRoots`, `:1080 SetWorkingRoot`, `:1127 SetRoots`, `:1169 SetWorkingSet` | mutate in-session only; nothing persisted until commit |
| 5 | SQL database | `sqle/database.go:80 Database{ddb}`, `sqle/database_provider.go:99` | `sql.Database` impl; resolves tables against the session's `RootValue` |
| 6 | RootValue | `doltdb/root_val.go:531 GetTable`, `:471 GetTableHash`, storage in `root_val_storage.go` | table-name → table address map |
| 7 | Table | `doltdb/table.go:315 GetRowData`, `:319 GetRowDataWithDescriptors` | schema + row/index addresses |
| 8 | Durable index | `doltdb/durable/index.go`, `artifact_index.go`, `proximity_index.go` | typed wrapper over the prolly map |
| 9 | Prolly tree | `store/prolly/`, `store/val/` | B-tree over `val.Tuple`s |
| 10 | NodeStore | `store/prolly/tree/node_store.go:37` — `Read :41`, `ReadMany :44`, `Write :47`, `PurgeCaches :62` | node cache over the ChunkStore; `PurgeCaches` exists specifically so GC can drop post-collection stale nodes |
| 11 | ValueStore | `store/types/value_store.go` | chunk (de)serialization, GC coordination |
| 12 | ChunkStore | `store/chunks/chunk_store.go:74` | `Get :77`, `GetMany :82`, `Put :97` |
| 13 | NBS | `store/nbs/store.go:1102 Get`, `:1169 GetMany`, `:976 Put`, `:1510 Rebase` | memtable → novel tables → persisted tables |
| 14 | On disk | `store/nbs/journal*.go`, table files, `manifest*` | chunk journal + table files; manifest holds the root |

Read path skips the transaction machinery entirely — steps 6→14 are pure content-addressed reads off immutable hashes. **All the concurrency semantics live in steps 2 and A** (root pin at tx start, CAS at tx commit).

## D. The write/commit contract end-to-end

`dsess/transactions.go:384 doCommit` is the choke point a rework must preserve:

1. Look up `startPoint := tx.dbStartPoints[normalizedDbName]` (`:404`) — the root pinned at tx start.
2. `startPoint.db.ResolveWorkingSetAtRoot(ctx, ws.Ref(), startPoint.rootHash)` (`:409`) — read the *base* state as of that pinned root.
3. Loop up to `maxTxCommitRetries` (`:419`), holding a per-`(db, working-set-ref)` lock: `sess.Provider().TxLocks().Lock(ctx, lockID)` (`:421-427`), `lockID = dbName \x00 wsRef` (`:417`).
4. Re-read the *current* working set (`:432`); if unchanged vs `startState` → fast-forward, else `mergeRoots :508` (three-way merge of working/staged).
5. `writeFn(... startPoint.db, startState, commit, ws, existingWSHash, mergeOpts)` → `DoltDB.UpdateWorkingSet`/`CommitWithWorkingSet` → `datas.database.update :815` → `rt.Commit` → `nbs.commit`.
6. `datas.ErrOptimisticLockFailed` (`:459`) is caught and retried; exhaustion returns it to the caller (`:499`).

So the contract has **three stacked optimistic-concurrency levels**: `existingWSHash` compare-and-set at the dataset layer, `(newRoot, oldRoot)` CAS at the noms-root layer, and `upstream.root != last` + table-set CAS inside NBS. Plus one pessimistic lock (`TxLocks`) purely as a contention optimizer — correctness does not depend on it, but throughput does.

## E. Invariants a teammate must not break

1. **Root reads must be re-fetched inside the CAS loop** (`database_common.go:825` is inside `for`). Hoisting it out turns lost-update detection into silent data loss.
2. **A pinned root hash is only meaningful against the `DoltDB` it came from** — `dbRoot` (`transactions.go:92`) stores `db` *and* `rootHash` together for exactly this reason. If the rework makes `DoltDB` swappable/reopenable, a stale pin against a new instance resolves to garbage or a spurious not-found.
3. **`Rebase` is a mutation of visible state**, not a read (`doltdb.go:1953`). It moves `nbs.upstream` under running readers. Note `nbs.commit :1583` calls `rebase` on the no-op path — a "nothing changed" commit still advances what this process can see.
4. **GC coordination is woven through all three layers** — `keeperFunc`/`waitForGC` (`nbs/store.go:1573`), `waitForNotFinalizingGC` (`value_store.go:543`), `NodeStore.PurgeCaches` (`node_store.go:62`), `doltdb.GC :1967` and `doltdb/gcctx/gc_safepoint_controller.go`. Any retained handle to a chunk store or cached node that outlives a safepoint is a use-after-GC.
5. **`ChunkStoreFromDatabase` + `ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB` are the two sanctioned escapes** and both are exported. They are where Task 3's "holders that never name `*doltdb.DoltDB`" will be found.

```go
                                       // value_store.go:188 → lvs.cs
```

Call sites in `doltdb.go`: `:240` (NomsRoot), `:244` (AccessMode), `:249` (CommitRoot), `:253` (Has), `:357` (Teardown), `:945` (push concurrency control), `:1156` (CommitDangling), `:1954` (Rebase), `:2074-2075` and `:2117-2118` (pull/push src+dest), `:2126` (`chunks.TableFileStore` type assertion), `:2134/:2144/:2159/:2179` (table-file ops). Plus `CSMetricsSummary :256` via `datas.GetCSStatSummaryForDB`.

Consequences of the current contract:
- The store is reached through **three hops of unexported plumbing** (`Database.chunkStore()` is unexported; `ChunkStoreFromDatabase` is the only door), so a rework can change the interior — but `ChunkStoreFromDatabase` is exported and callable on any `datas.Database`, so external holders can bypass `DoltDB` entirely.
- `ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB` (`doltdb.go:172` region, comment says "Generally avoid this function") hands out the raw `datas.Database` — combined with the above, that is a full escape hatch to the ChunkStore.
- The `chunks.TableFileStore` assertion at `:2126` means the *dynamic* type of the store is load-bearing; any wrapper/decorator inserted by the rework breaks table-file push/pull and clone silently unless it forwards the assertion.

## C. Query → bytes: the full stack

| # | Layer | file:line | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SQL session | `sqle/dsess/session.go:348 StartTransaction`, `:446 CommitTransaction` | per-connection entry; pins a snapshot |
| 2 | Transaction snapshot | `sqle/dsess/transactions.go:86 DoltTransaction` / `:92 dbRoot{db, dbName, rootHash}` | **the noms root is captured per-DB at tx start** (`NewDoltTransaction :102`, `db.DbData().Ddb.NomsRoot(ctx)` at `:110`) — this is Dolt's MVCC |
| 3 | Branch session state | `sqle/dsess/database_session_state.go:102 branchState` | holds `workingSet`, `headCommit`, `headRoot`, `writeSession`, `dbData` per branch |
| 4 | Session root accessors | `session.go:992 GetRoots`, `:1080 SetWorkingRoot`, `:1127 SetRoots`, `:1169 SetWorkingSet` | mutate in-session only; nothing persisted until commit |
| 5 | SQL database | `sqle/database.go:80 Database{ddb}`, `sqle/database_provider.go:99` | `sql.Database` impl; resolves tables against the session's `RootValue` |
| 6 | RootValue | `doltdb/root_val.go:531 GetTable`, `:471 GetTableHash`, storage in `root_val_storage.go` | table-name → table address map |
| 7 | Table | `doltdb/table.go:315 GetRowData`, `:319 GetRowDataWithDescriptors` | schema + row/index addresses |
| 8 | Durable index | `doltdb/durable/index.go`, `artifact_index.go`, `proximity_index.go` | typed wrapper over the prolly map |
| 9 | Prolly tree | `store/prolly/`, `store/val/` | B-tree over `val.Tuple`s |
| 10 | NodeStore | `store/prolly/tree/node_store.go:37` — `Read :41`, `ReadMany :44`, `Write :47`, `PurgeCaches :62` | node cache over the ChunkStore; `PurgeCaches` exists specifically so GC can drop post-collection stale nodes |
| 11 | ValueStore | `store/types/value_store.go` | chunk (de)serialization, GC coordination |
| 12 | ChunkStore | `store/chunks/chunk_store.go:74` | `Get :77`, `GetMany :82`, `Put :97` |
| 13 | NBS | `store/nbs/store.go:1102 Get`, `:1169 GetMany`, `:976 Put`, `:1510 Rebase` | memtable → novel tables → persisted tables |
| 14 | On disk | `store/nbs/journal*.go`, table files, `manifest*` | chunk journal + table files; manifest holds the root |

Read path skips the transaction machinery entirely — steps 6→14 are pure content-addressed reads off immutable hashes. **All the concurrency semantics live in steps 2 and A** (root pin at tx start, CAS at tx commit).

## D. The write/commit contract end-to-end

`dsess/transactions.go:384 doCommit` is the choke point a rework must preserve:

1. Look up `startPoint := tx.dbStartPoints[normalizedDbName]` (`:404`) — the root pinned at tx start.
2. `startPoint.db.ResolveWorkingSetAtRoot(ctx, ws.Ref(), startPoint.rootHash)` (`:409`) — read the *base* state as of that pinned root.
3. Loop up to `maxTxCommitRetries` (`:419`), holding a per-`(db, working-set-ref)` lock: `sess.Provider().TxLocks().Lock(ctx, lockID)` (`:421-427`), `lockID = dbName \x00 wsRef` (`:417`).
4. Re-read the *current* working set (`:432`); if unchanged vs `startState` → fast-forward, else `mergeRoots :508` (three-way merge of working/staged).
5. `writeFn(... startPoint.db, startState, commit, ws, existingWSHash, mergeOpts)` → `DoltDB.UpdateWorkingSet`/`CommitWithWorkingSet` → `datas.database.update :815` → `rt.Commit` → `nbs.commit`.
6. `datas.ErrOptimisticLockFailed` (`:459`) is caught and retried; exhaustion returns it to the caller (`:499`).

So the contract has **three stacked optimistic-concurrency levels**: `existingWSHash` compare-and-set at the dataset layer, `(newRoot, oldRoot)` CAS at the noms-root layer, and `upstream.root != last` + table-set CAS inside NBS. Plus one pessimistic lock (`TxLocks`) purely as a contention optimizer — correctness does not depend on it, but throughput does.

## E. Invariants a teammate must not break

1. **Root reads must be re-fetched inside the CAS loop** (`database_common.go:825` is inside `for`). Hoisting it out turns lost-update detection into silent data loss.
2. **A pinned root hash is only meaningful against the `DoltDB` it came from** — `dbRoot` (`transactions.go:92`) stores `db` *and* `rootHash` together for exactly this reason. If the rework makes `DoltDB` swappable/reopenable, a stale pin against a new instance resolves to garbage or a spurious not-found.
3. **`Rebase` is a mutation of visible state**, not a read (`doltdb.go:1953`). It moves `nbs.upstream` under running readers. Note `nbs.commit :1583` calls `rebase` on the no-op path — a "nothing changed" commit still advances what this process can see.
4. **GC coordination is woven through all three layers** — `keeperFunc`/`waitForGC` (`nbs/store.go:1573`), `waitForNotFinalizingGC` (`value_store.go:543`), `NodeStore.PurgeCaches` (`node_store.go:62`), `doltdb.GC :1967` and `doltdb/gcctx/gc_safepoint_controller.go`. Any retained handle to a chunk store or cached node that outlives a safepoint is a use-after-GC.
5. **`ChunkStoreFromDatabase` + `ExposeDatabaseFromDoltDB` are the two sanctioned escapes** and both are exported. They are where Task 3's "holders that never name `*doltdb.DoltDB`" will be found.

// patchState is the state for the patch workflow.
type patchState struct {
	sqlCtx                *sql.Context
	queryist              cli.Queryist
	tables                []string
	changeCounts          map[string]*tablePatchInfo
	currentTable          string
	currentTableSchema    sql.Schema
	currentTableLastRowId int
	currentRowId          int
	currentRow            sql.Row
	err                   error
}

// stageCurrentChange stages the current change. "a" command.
func (ps *patchState) stageCurrentChange(c *ishell.Context) {
	qry := fmt.Sprintf("UPDATE dolt_workspace_%s SET staged = TRUE WHERE id = %d", ps.currentTable, ps.currentRowId)
	_, iter, _, err := ps.queryist.Query(ps.sqlCtx, qry)
	if err != nil {
		ps.err = err
		c.Stop()
		return
	}

content.text:
Here is the full chain, in execution order.

## Hop 0 — The abstraction being handed out

`cli.Queryist` — `go/cmd/dolt/cli/command.go:103`. Two methods: `Query(*sql.Context, string)` and `QueryWithBindings(...)`. This is the *only* thing a CLI command is supposed to hold; it deliberately hides whether the work happens in-process or over a socket.

`cli.CliContext` — `cli/cli_context.go:60` — is the carrier: `QueryEngine(ctx, opts...) (QueryEngineResult, error)` at `:64`, plus `Close()` at `:68`.

## Hop 1 — Process entry decides *which* kind of access

`cmd/dolt/dolt.go:531 initCliContext(cfg.subCommand)` gates the whole thing; commands not in that set (`init`, etc.) get a `nil` CliContext and never touch SQL.

`cmd/dolt/dolt.go:541` → `buildLateBinder(ctx, cfg.cwdFs, dEnv, mrEnv, creds, cfg.apr, cfg.subCommand, verboseEngineSetup)` — `dolt.go:636`. It **selects the backend but constructs nothing**:

- `dolt.go:672` — `--host` given → `sqlserver.BuildConnectionStringQueryist(...)` → **remote** path.
- `dolt.go:718` — no valid repository → returns a binder that only produces an error, deferred so `--help` still works.
- `dolt.go:730` — `targetEnv == nil` → falls back to `rootEnv`; then `targetEnv.IsAccessModeReadOnly(ctx)` at `:735` decides whether to look for an already-running server.
- otherwise → local engine path (Hop 3).

## Hop 2 — Wrapped in the caching holder

`cmd/dolt/dolt.go:548` → `cli.NewCliContext(cfg.apr, dEnv.Config, cfg.cwdFs, lateBind)` — `cli/cli_context.go:70`. Builds `LateBindCliContext` (`:97`) with `activeContext: &QueryistContext{}` (`:81`) and `bind: latebind` (`:82`).

`LateBindCliContext` is passed **by value** but `activeContext` is a `*QueryistContext` (`:101`) — the pointer indirection is what makes the cache survive value copies. That is load-bearing and easy to break.

## Hop 3 — Local construction: `LateBindQueryist` → `*SqlEngine`

Type: `cli.LateBindQueryist` — `cli/cli_context.go:57`, returns `LateBindQueryistResult{Queryist, Context, IsRemote, Closer}` (`:29`).

Local producer chain:

1. `cmd/dolt/commands/utils.go:131 BuildSqlEngineQueryist` — resolves `doltcfg` dir, privileges file, branch-control file, target db/branch (`:216-226`).
2. `utils.go:228` → `newLateBindingEngine(cfgDirPath, privsFp, branchControlFilePath, creds, database, mrEnv)` — `utils.go:235`. Builds `engine.SqlEngineConfig` at `:243` and returns the closure at `:253`. **Nothing is opened yet** — `mrEnv` is captured, not resolved.
3. First call of the closure: `utils.go:257 mrEnv.ReloadDBs(ctx)` — the deferred DB load actually happens here.
4. `utils.go:266-271` — if `LateBindQueryistConfig.EnableAutoGC` (`cli_context.go:36`), installs `sqle.NewAutoGCController(...)` into the config.
5. `utils.go:273` → **`engine.NewSqlEngine(ctx, mrEnv, config)`** — `cmd/dolt/commands/engine/sqlengine.go:109`.

Inside `NewSqlEngine` — this is where `*doltdb.DoltDB` becomes reachable:

| Step | file:line | What |
|---|---|---|
| GC safepoint controller created and put on ctx | `sqlengine.go:127-128` | `gcctx.NewGCSafepointController()` |
| DB load params threaded into each env | `:136-145` | before any DB opens |
| **`CollectDBs(ctx, mrEnv)`** | `:153` → `engine/utils.go:31` | iterates envs |
| `newDatabase(ctx, name, dEnv)` | `engine/utils.go:37` → `:55` | `dEnv.DbData(ctx)` at `:56` |
| `dEnv.DbData(ctx)` | `env/environment.go:929` | `Ddb: dEnv.DoltDB(ctx)` at `:931` |
| **`dEnv.DoltDB(ctx)`** | `env/environment.go:119` | lazily `LoadDoltDB` at `:121`, returns `dEnv.doltDB` — **the core object enters the graph here** |
| `sqle.NewDatabase(ctx, name, dbdata, editor.Options{})` | `engine/utils.go:60` → `sqle/database.go:203` | `dbData.Ddb.SetCrashOnFatalError()` `:211`; `dsess.NewGlobalStateStoreForDb(ctx, name, dbData.Ddb)` `:212`; **retains `ddb: dbData.Ddb`** at `:221` |
| replication wrapping | `sqlengine.go:160` | `sqle.ApplyReplicationConfig` may replace `dbs` with `ReadReplicaDatabase` |
| cluster db appended | `:182-186` | `all` = dbs + `dolt_cluster` |
| provider built | `:194` | `factory.NewProvider(b, mrEnv.FileSystem(), all, locations, ...)` |
| provider unwrapped | `:202-210` | must be or wrap `*sqle.DoltDatabaseProvider` |
| gms engine built | `:225` | `gms.New(analyzer...)` |
| session factory built | `:308` | `doltSessionFactory(pro, statsPro, mrEnv.Config(), bcController, gcSafepointController, config.Autocommit, branchActivityTracker)` — `sqlengine.go:600` |
| **fields assigned** | `:309-313` | `sqlEngine.provider = pro`, `.dsessFactory = sessFactory`, `.ContextFactory = sqlContextFactory`, `.engine = engine`, `.fs = pro.FileSystem()` |

`SqlEngine` struct: `sqlengine.go:59` — `{provider *sqle.DoltDatabaseProvider; ContextFactory; dsessFactory; engine *gms.Engine; fs}`. It satisfies `cli.Queryist` via `Query :478` / `QueryWithBindings :482`, both trivial delegations to `se.engine`.

## Hop 4 — A session is minted and bound to a `*sql.Context`

Still inside the closure:

Here is the full chain, in execution order.

## Hop 0 — The abstraction being handed out

`cli.Queryist` — `go/cmd/dolt/cli/command.go:103`. Two methods: `Query(*sql.Context, string)` and `QueryWithBindings(...)`. This is the *only* thing a CLI command is supposed to hold; it deliberately hides whether the work happens in-process or over a socket.

`cli.CliContext` — `cli/cli_context.go:60` — is the carrier: `QueryEngine(ctx, opts...) (QueryEngineResult, error)` at `:64`, plus `Close()` at `:68`.

## Hop 1 — Process entry decides *which* kind of access

`cmd/dolt/dolt.go:531 initCliContext(cfg.subCommand)` gates the whole thing; commands not in that set (`init`, etc.) get a `nil` CliContext and never touch SQL.

`cmd/dolt/dolt.go:541` → `buildLateBinder(ctx, cfg.cwdFs, dEnv, mrEnv, creds, cfg.apr, cfg.subCommand, verboseEngineSetup)` — `dolt.go:636`. It **selects the backend but constructs nothing**:

- `dolt.go:672` — `--host` given → `sqlserver.BuildConnectionStringQueryist(...)` → **remote** path.
- `dolt.go:718` — no valid repository → returns a binder that only produces an error, deferred so `--help` still works.
- `dolt.go:730` — `targetEnv == nil` → falls back to `rootEnv`; then `targetEnv.IsAccessModeReadOnly(ctx)` at `:735` decides whether to look for an already-running server.
- otherwise → local engine path (Hop 3).

## Hop 2 — Wrapped in the caching holder

`cmd/dolt/dolt.go:548` → `cli.NewCliContext(cfg.apr, dEnv.Config, cfg.cwdFs, lateBind)` — `cli/cli_context.go:70`. Builds `LateBindCliContext` (`:97`) with `activeContext: &QueryistContext{}` (`:81`) and `bind: latebind` (`:82`).

`LateBindCliContext` is passed **by value** but `activeContext` is a `*QueryistContext` (`:101`) — the pointer indirection is what makes the cache survive value copies. That is load-bearing and easy to break.

## Hop 3 — Local construction: `LateBindQueryist` → `*SqlEngine`

Type: `cli.LateBindQueryist` — `cli/cli_context.go:57`, returns `LateBindQueryistResult{Queryist, Context, IsRemote, Closer}` (`:29`).

// hasFeature reports whether |f| appears in |md|'s advertised
// features list. FEATURE_UNSPECIFIED is treated as always absent.
// |md| may be nil.
func hasFeature(md *remotesapi.GetRepoMetadataResponse, f remotesapi.Feature) bool {
	if md == nil || f == remotesapi.Feature_FEATURE_UNSPECIFIED {
		return false
	}

content.text:
I have the full picture. Every holder found is transcribed below.

# Audit: everything that retains `*doltdb.DoltDB` or its storage layers

**Legend for how a holder reaches the core object.** `DoltDB` = `libraries/doltcore/doltdb/doltdb.go:126` (`db hooksDatabase` → `datas.Database` → `types.ValueStore.cs` → `chunks.ChunkStore` → `nbs.NomsBlockStore`).

---

## A. Inside the core object itself (`doltdb/`)

| # | file:line | Field | Reaches core object via |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | `doltdb/doltdb.go:127` | `db hooksDatabase` | the dataset/root-swap layer; **all** chunk access re-derives from here |
| A2 | `doltdb/doltdb.go:128` | `vrw types.ValueReadWriter` | second, independent handle to the same `ChunkStore` |
| A3 | `doltdb/doltdb.go:129` | `ns tree.NodeStore` | third, independent handle to the same `ChunkStore` |
| A4 | `doltdb/doltdb.go:139` | `commitCache *lru.Cache[hash.Hash, *OptionalCommit]` | **cache of materialized `*Commit`s, each carrying its own `vrw`+`ns`** — survives `Rebase`/GC; not purged by anything named `PurgeCaches` in this file |
| A5 | `doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:27` | embedded `datas.Database` | the wrapped noms db |
| A6 | `doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:28` | `db *DoltDB` | **reference cycle** — closed at `doltdb.go:163` (`ret.db.db = ret`); `DoltDB` ⇄ `hooksDatabase` are mutually retaining |
| A7 | `doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:29` | `hooks []CommitHook` | each hook's `Execute(ctx, ds, db *DoltDB)` re-receives the DB; hooks themselves hold DBs (see F) |
| A8 | `doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:30` | `rsc *ReplicationStatusController` | per-commit replication waiters |
| A9 | `doltdb/doltdb.go:710` | `TagResolver.ddb *DoltDB` | late-loading tag metadata; **explicitly designed to outlive the listing call** |
| A10 | `doltdb/commit_itr.go:42` | `commitItr[C].ddb *DoltDB` | long-lived commit-walk iterator |
| A11 | `doltdb/commit.go:49-50` | `Commit.vrw`, `Commit.ns` | every `*Commit` independently pins the stores |
| A12 | `doltdb/root_val.go:136-137` | `rootValue.vrw`, `rootValue.ns` | every `RootValue` independently pins the stores |
| A13 | `doltdb/tag.go:29` | `Tag.vrw` | tag value reader |
| A14 | `doltdb/durable/index.go:356-357` | `vrw`, `ns` | durable index handle |
| A15 | `doltdb/durable/table.go:129-130` | `vrw`, `ns` | durable table handle |
| A16 | `doltdb/branch_activity.go:69` | `BranchActivityTracker` (`readTimes`/`writeTimes`/`activityChan`) | no DB handle, but a per-engine goroutine keyed by db+branch that outlives databases — stale-entry leak on db drop |

**A11–A15 are the silent multiplier**: a `RootValue`, `Commit`, or `durable.Index` cached anywhere (see E, F) keeps the old `ChunkStore` alive even if the `DoltDB` is swapped.

---

## B. `env/` — the CLI-side owner

| # | file:line | Field | Reaches core object via |
|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | `env/environment.go:92` | `DoltEnv.doltDB *doltdb.DoltDB` | **the canonical owner**; lazily loaded behind `loadDBOnce sync.Once` at `:91`; `Close()` at `:129` nils it but does *not* reset the Once |
| B2 | `env/environment.go:856` | `repoStateReader{*DoltEnv}` | embeds the whole env → `doltDB` |
| B3 | `env/environment.go:878` | `repoStateWriter{*DoltEnv}` | embeds the whole env → `doltDB` |
| B4 | `env/repo_state.go:60` | `DbData[C].Ddb *doltdb.DoltDB` | **the struct passed to nearly every action/command**; copied by value everywhere |
| B5 | `env/remotes.go:194` | `PushOptions.SrcDb *doltdb.DoltDB` | push source |
| B6 | `env/remotes.go:195` | `PushOptions.DestDb *doltdb.DoltDB` | push destination (a *second*, remote DoltDB) |
| B7 | `env/multi_repo_env.go:45` | `NamedEnv.env *DoltEnv` | → B1 |
| B8 | `env/multi_repo_env.go:50` | `MultiRepoEnv.envs []NamedEnv` | **holds every database in the process**; `ReloadDBs` mutates it under running engines |
| B9 | `env/grpc_dial_provider.go:67` | `dEnv *DoltEnv` | → B1, for remote dialing |
| B10 | `env/actions/commitwalk/commitwalk.go:32` | `doltDBResolver.ddb *doltdb.DoltDB` | adapter retained by commit-graph walks |

---

## C. `dbfactory/` — process-global caches ⚠️ highest risk

| # | file:line | Field | Reaches core object via |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | `dbfactory/file.go:90` | `singletonDB.ddb datas.Database` | **never names `DoltDB`** — but this *is* the db every local `DoltDB` is built on |
| C2 | `dbfactory/file.go:91` | `singletonDB.vrw types.ValueReadWriter` | same store |
| C3 | `dbfactory/file.go:92` | `singletonDB.ns tree.NodeStore` | same store |
| C4 | `dbfactory/file.go:96` | `var singletons = make(map[string]singletonDB)` | **package-level global, keyed by path, lives for the process**. `CreateDB` returns the cached triple at `:162`, so *two `DoltDB`s for the same path share one `ChunkStore`*. Escape hatches: `DisableSingletonCacheParam` (`:66`, checked `:152`), `CloseAllLocalDatabases` (`:98`), `DeleteFromSingletonCache` (`:110`) |
| C5 | `dbfactory/git_remote.go:100-102` | `gitRemoteCacheEntry{db datas.Database; vrw; ns}` | git-backed remote's db/store triple |
| C6 | `dbfactory/git_remote.go:107` | `var gitRemoteCache = map[string]gitRemoteCacheEntry{}` | second process-global cache (mutex at `:106`); drained only by `TeardownGitRemotes` (`:110`) |
| C7 | `dbfactory/git_remote.go:126` | `gitSharedChunkStore{*nbs.NomsBlockStore}` | **`Close()` at `:129` is a deliberate no-op** — this store cannot be closed by normal teardown; only `ForceClose` (called at `:120`) releases it |

C4 and C6 mean a lifecycle rework that assumes "one `DoltDB` ⇒ one store, closed when the DoltDB closes" is **already wrong today**.

---

## D. `sqle/` — SQL databases and the provider

| # | file:line | Field | Reaches core object via |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | `sqle/database.go:80` | `Database.ddb *doltdb.DoltDB` | the primary SQL-side handle |
| D2 | `sqle/database.go:116` | `TableResolver.db Database` | **never names `DoltDB`** → D1 |
| D3 | `sqle/database.go:130` | `ReadOnlyDatabase{Database}` | embeds D1 |
| D4 | `sqle/read_replica_database.go:40` | `ReadReplicaDatabase.srcDB *doltdb.DoltDB` | the **remote** DoltDB pulled from |
| D5 | `sqle/read_replica_database.go:43` | `ReadReplicaDatabase{Database}` | embeds D1 → local DoltDB. `DoltDatabases()` at `:113` returns *both* |
| D6 | `sqle/database_provider.go:68` | `databases map[string]dsess.SqlDatabase` | **never names `DoltDB`** — the registry of every live SQL database → D1/D5 |
| D7 | `sqle/database_provider.go:99` | `gitRemotes map[string]*doltdb.DoltDB` | second registry, guarded by `gitRemotesMu :100`; reset wholesale at `:353` (leaks the old DBs unless closed) |
| D8 | `sqle/database_provider.go:71` | `dbLocations map[string]filesys.Filesys` | per-db filesystem root, must stay in sync with D6 |
| D9 | `sqle/database_provider.go:64` | `dbLoadParams map[string]interface{}` | **config that only takes effect before the DoltDB is loaded** (comment at `:63`) — a rework that changes load ordering silently drops these |
| D10 | `sqle/tables.go:89` | `DoltTable.db dsess.SqlDatabase` | **never names `DoltDB`** → D1 |
| D11 | `sqle/tables.go:90` | `DoltTable.lockedToRoot doltdb.RootValue` | pins a `RootValue` → A12 → old store |
| D12 | `sqle/tables.go:664` | `WritableDoltTable.db Database` | → D1 |
| D13 | `sqle/tables.go:663` | `WritableDoltTable.pinnedWriteSession dsess.WriteSession` | pinned writer holding a working set |

# The ten holders most likely to break your rework

1. **`dbfactory/file.go:96 singletons`** — process-global; two `DoltDB`s for one path *already* share a `ChunkStore`. Any "close the DoltDB ⇒ close the store" invariant is unenforceable without going through `DeleteFromSingletonCache` (`:110`).
2. **`dbfactory/git_remote.go:126 gitSharedChunkStore.Close()` is a no-op** (`:129`). A store that ignores `Close` will survive your teardown.
3. **`dsess/session_cache.go:62` comment is factually wrong** — it asserts cached databases hold no data handles, justifying the cache's safety. `sqle.Database.ddb` (D1) makes it false. Fix the comment or the design; don't preserve the assumption.
4. **`dsess/transactions.go:93 dbRoot{db, rootHash}`** — the root pin is only meaningful against *that* `*DoltDB` instance. Make `DoltDB` swappable and every in-flight transaction resolves against the wrong storage.
5. **`sqle/commit_hooks.go:110 ch chan PushArg`** (buffer 2048) — up to 2048 `srcDb`/`destDb` pairs sitting in a channel, outliving database drops.
6. **`sqle/auto_gc.go:136 autoGCWork.db`** — same shape: a `*DoltDB` queued on a channel awaiting a GC slot.
7. **`doltdb/doltdb.go:139 commitCache`** — an LRU of `*Commit`s, each holding `vrw`+`ns` (A11). Nothing in `Rebase` (`:1953`) or `GC` (`:1967`) invalidates it.
8. **`doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:28 db *DoltDB`** — the `DoltDB` ⇄ `hooksDatabase` cycle established at `doltdb.go:163`. Any scheme that reconstructs one half must re-establish this or leave a dangling back-pointer.
9. **`remotesrv/dbcache.go:30-31 RemoteSrvStore`** and the wrappers over it (H16, H18, J7, J8) — chunk stores travel as narrow interfaces and get decorated. `doltdb.go:2126`'s `chunks.TableFileStore` type assertion breaks silently on any new wrapper.
10. **`sqle/database_provider.go:64 dbLoadParams` / `statspro/controller.go:78 dbLoadParams`** — both are documented as effective *only before* the DoltDB loads. Reordering load in the rework drops them with no error.

### Two structural notes

- **The interface-typed holders are ~40% of the total.** The set that never mentions `*doltdb.DoltDB`: `dsess.SqlDatabase` (D6, E37-E40, D10), `sql.Database` (E44-E56, F15), `Database` (D2, D3, D12, E2-E5, E7, E10-E13, E15), `cli.Queryist` (L1, L4, L5, L13), `datas.Database` (C1, C5), `remotesrv.RemoteSrvStore`/`DBCache` (H13-H24, L24), `chunks.ChunkStore` (G14, G17, H29, J8-J12), `tree.NodeStore`/`types.ValueReadWriter` (all of K, E41-E43), `DoltDatabaseProvider` (F1, I3). A `grep '\*doltdb\.DoltDB'` finds none of them.
- **There are three independent root-tracking sites**, not one: `nbs.upstream.root` (`store/nbs/store.go:1548`), `dbRoot.rootHash` in each transaction (F24), and `DoltChunkStore.root` on the remote path (H27). A rework of "how the storage root is tracked" must account for all three.

# The ten holders most likely to break your rework

1. **`dbfactory/file.go:96 singletons`** — process-global; two `DoltDB`s for one path *already* share a `ChunkStore`. Any "close the DoltDB ⇒ close the store" invariant is unenforceable without going through `DeleteFromSingletonCache` (`:110`).
2. **`dbfactory/git_remote.go:126 gitSharedChunkStore.Close()` is a no-op** (`:129`). A store that ignores `Close` will survive your teardown.
3. **`dsess/session_cache.go:62` comment is factually wrong** — it asserts cached databases hold no data handles, justifying the cache's safety. `sqle.Database.ddb` (D1) makes it false. Fix the comment or the design; don't preserve the assumption.
4. **`dsess/transactions.go:93 dbRoot{db, rootHash}`** — the root pin is only meaningful against *that* `*DoltDB` instance. Make `DoltDB` swappable and every in-flight transaction resolves against the wrong storage.
5. **`sqle/commit_hooks.go:110 ch chan PushArg`** (buffer 2048) — up to 2048 `srcDb`/`destDb` pairs sitting in a channel, outliving database drops.
6. **`sqle/auto_gc.go:136 autoGCWork.db`** — same shape: a `*DoltDB` queued on a channel awaiting a GC slot.
7. **`doltdb/doltdb.go:139 commitCache`** — an LRU of `*Commit`s, each holding `vrw`+`ns` (A11). Nothing in `Rebase` (`:1953`) or `GC` (`:1967`) invalidates it.
8. **`doltdb/hooksdatabase.go:28 db *DoltDB`** — the `DoltDB` ⇄ `hooksDatabase` cycle established at `doltdb.go:163`. Any scheme that reconstructs one half must re-establish this or leave a dangling back-pointer.
9. **`remotesrv/dbcache.go:30-31 RemoteSrvStore`** and the wrappers over it (H16, H18, J7, J8) — chunk stores travel as narrow interfaces and get decorated. `doltdb.go:2126`'s `chunks.TableFileStore` type assertion breaks silently on any new wrapper.
10. **`sqle/database_provider.go:64 dbLoadParams` / `statspro/controller.go:78 dbLoadParams`** — both are documented as effective *only before* the DoltDB loads. Reordering load in the rework drops them with no error.

### Two structural notes

- **The interface-typed holders are ~40% of the total.** The set that never mentions `*doltdb.DoltDB`: `dsess.SqlDatabase` (D6, E37-E40, D10), `sql.Database` (E44-E56, F15), `Database` (D2, D3, D12, E2-E5, E7, E10-E13, E15), `cli.Queryist` (L1, L4, L5, L13), `datas.Database` (C1, C5), `remotesrv.RemoteSrvStore`/`DBCache` (H13-H24, L24), `chunks.ChunkStore` (G14, G17, H29, J8-J12), `tree.NodeStore`/`types.ValueReadWriter` (all of K, E41-E43), `DoltDatabaseProvider` (F1, I3). A `grep '\*doltdb\.DoltDB'` finds none of them.
- **There are three independent root-tracking sites**, not one: `nbs.upstream.root` (`store/nbs/store.go:1548`), `dbRoot.rootHash` in each transaction (F24), and `DoltChunkStore.root` on the remote path (H27). A rework of "how the storage root is tracked" must account for all three.
